question: Based on Table 5.3-1 parameters and Figure 5.3-1, is the RPV design adequate for containing the RCS at design conditions?

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module's reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV is a critical pressure-retaining component that forms part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, supporting various internal systems and ensuring the integrity of the plant over its design lifetime through the use of materials compliant with ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards.

nuscale_ch05_0000.title: Preface
nuscale_ch05_0000.summary: The Preface outlines the copyright and usage restrictions for the NuScale Standard Plant Design Certification Application, specifically Chapter Five on the Reactor Coolant System and Connecting Systems. It specifies that only the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) is authorized to use, copy, or disclose the document as needed for regulatory purposes, with all copies required to include the copyright notice.

nuscale_ch05_0017.title: Reactor Vessel Integrity
nuscale_ch05_0017.summary: This section outlines the procedures and regulatory compliance measures to ensure the integrity of the NuScale reactor vessel over its 60-year design life. It details the development of operating procedures to prevent exceeding pressure-temperature limits, evaluates the vessel’s resistance to pressurized thermal shock and upper-shelf energy degradation, and confirms that predicted material properties remain within safety criteria throughout the vessel’s operational lifespan.

nuscale_ch05_0016.title: Pressure-Temperature Limits, Pressurized Thermal Shock, and Charpy Upper-Shelf Energy Data and Analyses
nuscale_ch05_0016.summary: This section outlines the basis for establishing operational pressure and temperature limits for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) to comply with regulatory requirements over the plant’s 60-year lifespan. It describes the methodology used to calculate pressure-temperature limit curves, accounting for vessel embrittlement, and provides the relevant figures and tables that define acceptable operating conditions to prevent non-ductile failure of the reactor vessel.

nuscale_ch05_0007.title: Integrity of Reactor Coolant Boundary
nuscale_ch05_0007.summary: This section describes the design features of the NuScale Power Module (NPM) that ensure the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) throughout the plant's design life. It details the components and systems included in the RCPB, such as the reactor pressure vessel, containment isolation valves, safety valves, and associated piping, all designed to meet regulatory definitions and maintain pressure containment.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with NRC regulations and ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) requirements for overpressure protection of the reactor coolant boundary. It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and applied according to specific regulatory guides and federal regulations, ensuring all conditions and provisions are met. A consolidated list of the specific code cases used is provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the NuScale Power Module’s Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems, including the reactor pressure vessel, steam generators, safety and emergency valves, and associated piping. It outlines the integration of these components within the reactor vessel and specifies the boundaries between the primary and secondary sides, emphasizing the role of the steam generators and their connection to the RCS pressure boundary.

nuscale_ch05_0008.title: Compliance with Codes and Code Cases
nuscale_ch05_0008.summary: This section describes how the NuScale Power Module (NPM) and its reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components comply with relevant U.S. regulations, specifically 10 CFR 50.55a and associated General Design Criteria. It details that these components are designed, fabricated, and tested to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Section III standards, with specific classifications for different piping systems, and confirms the use of the 2013 Edition of the ASME BPVC as the code of record.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer in the NuScale reactor is integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and functions to regulate reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering unnecessary reactor trips or safety system actuations. Instrumentation continuously monitors pressure and water level, providing data to plant control systems.

nuscale_ch05_0020.title: Steam Generators
nuscale_ch05_0020.summary: The steam generators (SGs) in the NuScale Power Module are integrated within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which also houses other key components such as the pressurizer, safety valves, and emergency cooling systems. The SG system includes feedwater and main steam piping, plenum access ports, and thermal relief valves, with the RPV itself forming the outer pressure boundary for the SGs and their associated components.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale reactor pressurizer, specifying the alloys and standards for each component, such as the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, and bolting. It emphasizes the use of corrosion-resistant materials in accordance with ASME BPVC Section II specifications to ensure compatibility and durability in the reactor coolant system.

nuscale_ch05_0010.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Materials
nuscale_ch05_0010.summary: The Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) materials, including welds, are selected and fabricated in accordance with ASME BPVC standards and meet the regulatory requirements of 10 CFR 50, including General Design Criteria for quality, environmental compatibility, and fracture prevention. The materials used for Class 1 components, such as the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and steam generators (SGs), are specified in Table 5.2-4, ensuring compliance with all relevant material and testing standards.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) does not use traditional components with designed leakage rates, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using installed instrumentation. Any leakage into containment, regardless of source, quickly vaporizes and disperses, so all leakage is treated as unidentified until specifically located and quantified by other means; the leakage rate is determined by RCS inventory balance and compared to the containment evacuation system (CES) flow. The leakage detection systems are designed to be reliable, redundant, and sensitive enough to support leak-before-break analyses.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat during non-LOCA events when normal cooling is unavailable, ensuring safe shutdown of the NuScale Power Module without external power. The DHRS maintains reactor coolant system temperatures below 420°F within 36 hours after an event, protecting fuel and pressure boundaries, and its reliability and risk significance are evaluated per regulatory guidelines.

edges.description: Section A provides specific technical details about the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) that are part of the broader system and documentation introduced in Section B's preface to the chapter.

Section A describes the physical design and function of the RPV as a pressure-retaining component of the RCS, while Section B addresses the operational procedures developed to ensure the RPV operates within pressure-temperature limits, connecting the vessel's design parameters to compliance with regulatory requirements.

Section A provides a general description of the RPV as the pressure-retaining component of the RCS, while Section B specifies compliance of RPV attachments and welds with ASME code requirements, which are necessary for ensuring the vessel's structural integrity under design conditions.

Section A provides the context and scope of the document, while Section B presents specific technical compliance details regarding RPV attachments and their examination per ASME BPVC requirements, which are necessary to assess whether the RPV design (as described in Table 5.3-1 and Figure 5.3-1) meets regulatory adequacy for containing the RCS at design conditions.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, which directly relates to Section B's specification of those pressure-temperature limits and associated ASME code requirements for the RPV.

Section A defines the integrity requirements for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), which includes the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) described in Section B as a key pressure-retaining component of the reactor coolant system (RCS), thus establishing that the RPV's adequacy is directly relevant to satisfying the RCPB integrity requirements.

Section A provides specific technical information about the integrity and design features of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, which is a detailed aspect referenced within the broader context of the NuScale Standard Plant Design Certification Application introduced in Section B.

Section A defines the integrity requirements for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), while Section B describes the development of operating procedures to ensure that the reactor vessel operates within the pressure-temperature limits specified for RCPB integrity, thus showing that B supports the implementation of A's requirements.

Section A outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR 50.55a) that the RPV must comply with for overpressure protection, while Section B describes the RPV as the pressure-retaining component of the RCS, thus connecting the regulatory requirements in A to the physical design described in B.

Section A references regulatory compliance and code cases relevant to the RPV design discussed in Section B's broader document context, establishing the regulatory framework for the design's adequacy analysis.

Section A outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR 50.55a) governing RPV overpressure protection, while Section B describes the implementation of operating procedures to ensure the RPV operates within those pressure-temperature limits, thus connecting the regulatory requirements to their operational fulfillment.

Section A describes the overall design and components of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), including the RPV, while Section B provides specific details about the RPV as a pressure-retaining component within the RCS, thus Section B specifies details for the general system described in Section A.
Section A provides specific technical details about the RPV and its design basis, which are elaborations of the general subject matter introduced in Section B's preface to the chapter.

Section A describes the RPV's design basis and components, while Section B addresses the requirement that operating procedures ensure the RPV's pressure-temperature limits (as defined by design) are not exceeded, thus linking the design parameters in A to the operational compliance required in B.

Section A states that the RPV and associated components are designed and constructed to meet ASME BPVC Class 1 requirements per 10 CFR 50.55a, while Section B describes the RPV as the pressure-retaining component of the RCS, thus connecting the regulatory compliance (A) to the physical system (B) whose adequacy is being analyzed.

Section A establishes that the RPV is designed and constructed to meet regulatory and ASME Code requirements, while Section B describes the development of operating procedures to ensure that the RPV operates within those design limits, thereby connecting compliance in design (A) with operational assurance (B).

Section A describes the pressurizer as an integral part of the reactor vessel, while Section B describes the reactor vessel as the pressure-retaining component of the reactor coolant system, connecting them by specifying that the pressurizer is a functional region within the reactor vessel relevant to RCS pressure containment.

Section A describes the design and function of the pressurizer as part of the reactor vessel, which is a component discussed in the broader context of the Reactor Coolant System introduced in Section B's preface.

Section A describes the pressurizer's role in maintaining RCS pressure, which directly affects the reactor vessel's pressure boundary discussed in Section B, where operating procedures ensure the vessel's integrity under design conditions.

Section A describes the design features ensuring the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B details compliance with ASME and NRC overpressure protection requirements that are necessary to maintain that integrity.

Section B describes the specific components and design basis of the RPV, which are referenced in Section A as part of the RCPB whose integrity must be maintained to contain the RCS at design conditions.

Section A describes the design intent to maintain RCPB integrity, while Section B specifies that this integrity is achieved by compliance with ASME Code and regulatory requirements, directly connecting the design features to their regulatory basis.

Section B describes the design and function of the pressurizer, which is an integral component of the reactor vessel discussed in Section A as part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary whose integrity must be maintained under design conditions.

Section A outlines the regulatory codes and standards (such as ASME BPVC and 10 CFR 50.55a) that must be complied with for overpressure protection, while Section B describes the physical design and components of the RPV and RCS, meaning Section A specifies the regulatory requirements that Section B's design must satisfy.

Section A provides specific details about overpressure protection provisions and references compliance with ASME BPVC and regulatory requirements, which are generally described in Section B as the overall compliance of the RPV and components with 10 CFR 50.55a and ASME BPVC codes.

Section A outlines the regulatory and code compliance requirements for overpressure protection of the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B describes the design and function of the pressurizer as an integral part of the reactor vessel, both of which are directly connected through their roles in maintaining RCS pressure within design limits as specified in Table 5.3-1 and Figure 5.3-1.

Section A describes the RPV's design features and components, while Section B states that the RPV is designed and constructed in compliance with regulatory codes (10 CFR 50.55a and ASME BPVC), thus Section A's design details are intended to satisfy Section B's regulatory requirements.

Section A describes the overall RPV design and its components, including the pressurizer, while Section B provides specific design details about the pressurizer as an integral part of the RPV, thus Section B specifies details for a component generally described in Section A.

Section A establishes that the RPV and associated components are designed and constructed to meet regulatory and ASME Code requirements, while Section B describes the pressurizer as an integral part of the RPV, indicating that its design and function are encompassed within the RPV's code compliance as referenced in Section A.

Section A describes the components and design basis of the RPV within the reactor coolant system, while Section B provides specific details about the RPV's role as a pressure-retaining component, with both sections connecting through their discussion of the RPV's function and containment of the RCS at design conditions.
Section A provides specific design details about the RPV and its components, which are elaborated upon in the general context set by Section B's preface to the chapter on the Reactor Coolant System.

Section A describes the components and design basis of the RPV, while Section B addresses the development of operating procedures to ensure the RPV's pressure-temperature limits (as specified in Table 5.3-1 and Figure 5.3-1) are not exceeded, thus connecting the design features to operational compliance.

Section A specifies the materials and components of the RPV upper shell and head, which are part of the pressurizer, while Section B provides the overall description of the RPV as the pressure-retaining component of the RCS, connecting the detailed material information in A to the general vessel function in B.

Section A references specific sections of the broader document introduced in Section B, indicating that Section A's detailed component descriptions are part of the overall design documentation outlined in Section B.

Section A describes the materials and components of the RPV upper shell and head, which are relevant to the RPV's ability to contain the RCS, while Section B discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, both addressing aspects of RPV integrity under design conditions.

Section A specifies the material standards and regulatory compliance for the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary, which includes the RPV described in Section B as the pressure-retaining component of the RCS, thus providing the detailed material basis for the vessel's adequacy discussed in Section B.
Section A references Section 5.3 for further details on RPV material conformance, which is part of the broader document introduced in Section B's preface.

Section A specifies that the RCPB materials, including those for the RPV, meet ASME and regulatory requirements, while Section B discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure that the RPV operates within pressure-temperature limits, thus both sections together address different aspects (material adequacy and operational control) necessary for the RPV to contain the RCS at design conditions.

Section A provides a general overview of the RPV and its components, while Section B specifies the materials and construction details of the pressurizer, which is part of the RPV described in Section A.

Section A describes the components and design basis of the RPV, while Section B confirms that the RPV materials meet ASME and regulatory requirements, thus supporting the adequacy of the RPV design for containing the RCS at design conditions.

Section A describes the specific components and materials of the RPV upper shell and head, while Section B establishes that these materials conform to ASME BPVC and regulatory requirements, thus Section A's material details are intended to satisfy the compliance criteria outlined in Section B.

Section B describes the RPV as the pressure-retaining component of the RCS, while Section A discusses the detection of leakage from the RCS boundary, connecting them through the integrity and monitoring of the RPV's ability to contain the RCS at design conditions.

Section A discusses the limitations of leakage detection for the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B describes the development of operating procedures to ensure pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, both addressing aspects of maintaining RPV integrity under design conditions.

Section B describes the RPV as the pressure-retaining component of the RCS, which is the system whose conditions must be contained by the RPV, while Section A discusses the DHRS's role in maintaining RCS conditions (such as pressure and temperature) within design limits during certain events, thus connecting the RPV's containment function to the system's operational parameters.

Section A describes the design basis and function of the Decay Heat Removal System, which is a subsystem detailed in the broader context introduced in Section B's preface to the Reactor Coolant System chapter.

Section A describes the DHRS's capability to remove decay heat and maintain RCS conditions within specified limits, while Section B addresses the development of operating procedures to ensure RPV pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, both connecting through the requirement to keep RCS parameters within safe design values during operation and transients.
